title: Personality traits
content: One of the interesting topics for exploration is connections between personality traits and patterns of behavior. Are they even connected? And, if yes, then how?

These sorts of data mappings are useful in practice, but I think we have to be careful. All these traits don't define you. They describe you. There is a difference. We assign the words to different observable qualities, but they don't necessarily explain the internal structure of the organism.

I will suggest a classification that can be used with my theory, but again, it's all about semantics and language. We can find the origins of different traits in the system, but they don't explain how the system works inside.

The first group of traits comes directly from our 16 patterns of behavior of the class I. When you use the same pattern over a long period of time, people start to associate its parts with you. Creative. Aggressive. Lazy. Dramatic. These words describe what you do. They portray your patterns of behavior. We easily apply these words to little kids and our pet animals.

The second group includes the traits based on your connections with others. They don't describe your exact actions, but rather add some data on the alignment of your behavior with a group. Selfless. Selfish. Reliable. Unreliable. These words naturally come in pairs, and they're relative by definition. If you have multiple groups to interact with, then it's very possible that they'll associate the opposite traits with you. And usually we don't apply these words to animals, unless they're seriously involved in our group activities, like hunting dogs.

The third group is about your efficiency. Strong. Cool-headed. Purposeful. Emotional. All over the place. These words don't describe any specific patterns or alliances, but provide data on your ability to get the job done and not waste the resources. We pay close attention to them in competitive environments, where the resources are limited. Interestingly enough, such mental qualities are often mentioned in traditional martial arts as something deeply interconnected with a state of a body and not with any specific behavior.

The fourth group includes the rules-based qualities. Idealistic. Principled. Methodical. Pedantic. In some cases they affect your efficiency, in some cases they don't, but what makes them special is that you need to be intelligent and create a lot of rules in your memory to get these traits associated with you. Usually we treat little kids and animals as intellectually inferior creatures and do not apply these words to them in any form.

What do you do? In whose interest? Are there any risks? Anything special? Answers to these questions describe your personality. In different societies we may use different words, but the questions are the same.
